name: Cedar Lebanon
description: The Cedar of Lebanon (Cedrus libani) holds immense cultural and mythological significance in the Middle East, particularly in Lebanon, where it is the national emblem. Revered for its strength, longevity, and fragrant wood, it was believed to be a gift from the gods. Ancient civilizations, including the Phoenicians, Egyptians, and Mesopotamians, prized cedar for its durability and used it extensively in shipbuilding, construction of temples and palaces, and crafting sarcophagi for pharaohs. The Bible mentions the Cedar of Lebanon numerous times, associating it with strength, prosperity, and divine favor. King Solomon famously used cedarwood in the construction of the First Temple in Jerusalem, symbolizing its sacred status. In Mesopotamian mythology, cedar was linked to immortality and the dwelling place of the gods. Gilgamesh, in his epic quest, traveled to the Cedar Forest, guarded by the monstrous Humbaba, to obtain its valuable timber. Burning cedar incense was a common practice in religious ceremonies, believed to purify spaces and invoke the presence of deities. The tree itself represents resilience, steadfastness, and the enduring spirit of the region. Its majestic presence and aromatic scent continue to evoke a sense of awe and reverence, reminding people of their connection to the land and their rich cultural heritage. The Cedar's resin was used medicinally, and its wood provided shelter and resources, further solidifying its importance in daily life and spiritual practices.
subtitle: Strength, longevity, and divine connection.

keyMyths.title: Gilgamesh and the Cedar Forest
keyMyths.description: Gilgamesh journeys to the Cedar Forest to obtain its timber, facing the guardian Humbaba. This myth highlights the cedar's value and the challenges associated with acquiring it.

keyMyths.title: Solomon's Temple
keyMyths.description: King Solomon used cedarwood from Lebanon to build the First Temple in Jerusalem. This symbolized the cedar's sacred status and its association with divine architecture.

extendedContent.title: Cedar in Mesopotamian Mythology
extendedContent.content: In Mesopotamian mythology, the Cedar of Lebanon was closely associated with the divine realm. The Epic of Gilgamesh recounts the hero's journey to the Cedar Forest, a realm guarded by the monstrous Humbaba, appointed by the gods to protect the sacred trees. Felling these cedars was considered an act of both bravery and sacrilege, symbolizing humanity's ambition to conquer nature and challenge divine authority. The cedar's strength and longevity mirrored the perceived immortality of the gods, making it a fitting material for constructing temples and palaces. The fragrant wood was also used in religious rituals, with its smoke believed to carry prayers to the heavens. The cedar forest, therefore, represented a liminal space between the mortal and divine worlds, a source of both immense power and potential danger.

extendedContent.title: Phoenician Trade and Symbolism
extendedContent.content: The Phoenicians, renowned seafarers and traders, heavily relied on the Cedar of Lebanon for shipbuilding and commerce. The durable and rot-resistant wood enabled them to construct sturdy vessels that navigated the Mediterranean Sea, establishing trade routes and cultural exchanges across the ancient world. Cedar became a symbol of Phoenician prosperity and maritime power. The trees were not merely a resource but an integral part of their identity, representing their connection to the land and their mastery of the seas. Cedar logs were highly sought after by neighboring civilizations, including the Egyptians and Israelites, contributing to Phoenicia's economic wealth and influence. The Phoenician alphabet itself may have been inscribed on cedar tablets, further solidifying the tree's importance in their cultural and intellectual life.

extendedContent.title: Cedar in Biblical and Religious Texts
extendedContent.content: The Cedar of Lebanon is mentioned numerous times in the Hebrew Bible, often symbolizing strength, majesty, and divine favor. King Solomon famously used cedarwood to construct the First Temple in Jerusalem, highlighting its sacred and prestigious status. The Book of Psalms compares the righteous to a flourishing cedar tree, emphasizing their resilience and spiritual growth. In prophetic literature, the felling of cedars is sometimes used as a metaphor for the downfall of powerful nations, signifying the transience of worldly power. The association of cedar with holiness and purity extended beyond Judaism, influencing early Christian symbolism. The tree's enduring nature and fragrant aroma were interpreted as symbols of Christ's incorruptibility and the sweet savor of salvation.

habitat: The Cedar of Lebanon is native to the mountains of the Eastern Mediterranean basin, including Lebanon, Syria, Turkey, and Cyprus. It thrives in mountainous regions with well-drained soil and a Mediterranean climate.
shortDescription: The Cedar of Lebanon is a culturally significant tree in Middle Eastern mythology, symbolizing strength, longevity, and divine favor, prominently featured in religious texts and ancient epics.
